title: MINISTRY OF EDUCATION — GTCI 2025 COMPREHENSIVE EDUCATION INDICATOR PLAYBOOK
subtitle: 18 indicators across 7 thematic groups — including all education-linked indicators. Brunei rank 43/135 | Singapore rank 1/135
quickWins: 1.2.6 Internet in Schools → Submit OGN/school connectivity data to UNESCO UIS (uis.stats@unesco.org) by March 2026 — score N/A → 75–85/100
3.1.2 Tertiary GER → Count overseas students as outbound in UNESCO — GER from 27% to ~45% IMMEDIATELY
3.1.4 PISA → Request PISA Diagnostic Report from OECD (pisa@oecd.org)
6.1.4 Researchers → Conduct national researcher census and submit UNESCO UIS Form R by June 2026
5.2.1 Skills Mismatch → Launch annual National Skills Demand Assessment aligned to Manpower Blueprint 2024 (rank 119/135 — most urgent)
3.1.4 PISA 2025 CONFIRMED — using Digital World domain; prep mock assessments NOW for Dec 2026 results

groups.label: 📡 GROUP 1: ICT INFRASTRUCTURE IN EDUCATION
groups.context: Internet connectivity and digital infrastructure in schools are foundational to 21st-century learning. MOE controls school ICT data through OGN (One Government Network, managed by EGNC). Score=0 on 1.2.6 is a pure data submission gap — data exists but has never been submitted.

indicators.name: Internet Access in Schools
indicators.definition: The share of primary schools with access to the internet via fixed narrowband, fixed broadband, or mobile networks.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE RESPONSIBILITY. Score=0 because Brunei has NEVER submitted school internet data to UNESCO UIS or WEF EOS. School connectivity data exists via OGN (EGNC, egnc.gov.bn) and MOE ICT Division already.
indicators.current_status: Score: N/A. Brunei not reporting. OGN (One Government Network) connects all government schools via EGNC (egnc.gov.bn). Singapore scores 100.00 (rank 1). Estimated Brunei score if reported: 75–85/100.
indicators.action_plan:
STEP 1 — UNESCO UIS SUBMISSION (PRIMARY FIX — MARCH ANNUALLY):
• MOE ICT Division to complete UNESCO UIS Annual Education Survey, Section 4: ICT in Education
• Contact: uis.stats@unesco.org | Deadline: 31 March each year
• Data to submit: % schools with internet/broadband >10 Mbps, student:device ratio
• Source: OGN (One Government Network) database (already at MOE ICT Division) — deadline March 2026 for GTCI 2027

STEP 2 — ITU QUESTIONNAIRE (OCTOBER ANNUALLY):
• MOE to provide AITI with annual school connectivity report by September
• AITI submits to ITU under 'ICT in Education' module (itustats@itu.int)

STEP 3 — CLOSE REMAINING INFRASTRUCTURE GAPS:
• 100% schools (including Temburong, interior Tutong/Belait) with minimum 100 Mbps fibre by end-2026
• Continue Smart Classrooms rollout per MOE Strategic Plan 2023-2027 Initiative 6
• Device-to-student ratio: target 1:1 for Years 7–12 by 2027
indicators.contact: UNESCO UIS: uis.stats@unesco.org | AITI (ITU): aiti.gov.bn | WEF EOS via BEDB: eos@weforum.org | MOE ICT Division: moe.gov.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: N/A → 75–85/100. Est. rank: 25–40/135. IMMEDIATE HIGHEST PRIORITY — data exists, zero effort to collect, huge score gain.
indicators.policy_rec: Annual School ICT Census + UNESCO UIS ICT in Education Submission (March)

groups.label: 🏫 GROUP 2: FORMAL EDUCATION SYSTEM (Core MOE Mandate)
groups.context: The Formal Education sub-pillar covers tertiary enrolment, expenditure, TVET, PISA, and university rankings — all directly under MOE governance. This is Brunei's weakest GTCI pillar (Grow: score 39.88, rank 55). MOE actions here have the highest aggregate impact on overall GTCI rank.

indicators.name: Vocational & TVET Enrolment Rate
indicators.definition: Enrolment in vocational/TVET programmes as % of total secondary education enrolment. Measures how well the education system directs students toward practical technical skills.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE RESPONSIBILITY. TVET enrolment and IBTE fall directly under MOE. IBTE is a statutory board under the Ministry of Education (moe.gov.bn). UNESCO UIS submission may be incomplete.
indicators.current_status: Score: 20.87/100, Rank: 56/135. Vocational enrolment as % of secondary. Low relative to peers.
indicators.action_plan:
• Expand mainstream secondary vocational streams from lower or upper secondary so more students are formally enrolled in vocational programmes while still counted within secondary education.

• Reclassify and map all existing applied, technical, BTEC, school-based skills, and pre-vocational programmes against ISCED orientation rules to ensure eligible students are reported to UNESCO UIS as vocational secondary enrolment.

• Prioritise sectors tied to Brunei's manpower blueprint and emerging economy needs, especially digital and green skills.

• Strengthen annual administrative reporting to UIS so eligible enrolment is not undercounted.
indicators.contact: UNESCO UIS: uis.stats@unesco.org | IBTE: ibte.edu.bn | MOE: via moe.gov.bn | Darussalam Enterprise: de.gov.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 20.87 → 35/100 by 2028. TVET enrolment: 30% of secondary by 2028. IBTE employment rate: >85% within 6 months.
indicators.policy_rec: Dual Training MOUs with 30 Companies + Annual IBTE Graduate Employment Report + Align IBTE Programmes with Manpower Blueprint 2024

indicators.name: Tertiary Gross Enrolment Ratio (GER)
indicators.primary_source: UNESCO Institute for Statistics / WIPO GII
indicators.definition: Gross tertiary enrolment ratio: students enrolled in tertiary education as a % of tertiary-age population. Key measure of higher education system capacity and access.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE RESPONSIBILITY. HIGHEST SINGLE IMPACT ACTION. Counting overseas scholarship students in UNESCO GER could improve rank from 85 to ~55 immediately — no new investment needed.
indicators.current_status: Score: 20.56/100, Rank: 85/135. GER ~27%. CRITICAL: 5,000–8,000 Brunei overseas students are NOT counted in UNESCO GER. This one data fix transforms this indicator.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Confirm current GER methodology and institutional coverage
MOE Higher Education Division and DP jointly review UNESCO UIS guidance on tertiary GER and confirm which Brunei-based institutions are currently included (UBD, UTB, UNISSA and other accredited providers) and how their enrolment data are compiled.

Step 2 – Strengthen annual data submissions to UNESCO UIS
Each year, MOE coordinates with all accredited tertiary institutions to verify enrolment figures (by programme type, level and gender) before these data are supplied to DEPS for inclusion in the UNESCO UIS education survey.

Step 3 – Use internal analysis to guide medium-term expansion decisions
Using the consolidated data, MOE periodically analyses tertiary participation by field of study, gender and institution, and compares it with labour-market information and the Manpower Blueprint to identify areas where modest, targeted expansion of places may be justified.
indicators.contact: UNESCO UIS: uis.stats@unesco.org | MOE Higher Education Division: moe.gov.bn | JPA Scholarship Division: jpa.gov.bn | UBD Registry: ubd.edu.bn | UTB Registry: utb.edu.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 20.55 → 40+/100 via data correction. GER: 27% → 40–45% immediately; 50% by 2030 with expansion. Rank: 85 → ~55/135.
indicators.policy_rec: Count all JPA/government overseas scholarship holders as outbound tertiary students in UNESCO UIS submission. One spreadsheet, one email — biggest immediate GTCI gain available.

indicators.name: Tertiary Education Expenditure per Student
indicators.definition: Government expenditure on tertiary education per student, normalised. Measures the financial resources dedicated per learner in higher education.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE RESPONSIBILITY. Brunei ranks 6th globally — a major strength. Must maintain rank as student denominator grows from 3.1.2 fix. Must update expenditure data simultaneously.
indicators.current_status: Score: 50.31/100, Rank: 6/135. Top-6 globally on per-student tertiary spending. Singapore ranks 16th (35.95) — Brunei spends MORE per student than Singapore. Key strength to leverage.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Clarify coverage and methodology of current expenditure data
MOE Higher Education Department reviews how Brunei's tertiary education expenditure is currently compiled for UNESCO UIS, including which institutions and spending categories are included.

Step 2 – Strengthen internal data flows across key agencies and institutions
MOE Higher Education Department coordinates with UBD, UTB, UNISSA, other accredited institutions and MoF to ensure that major tertiary-related funding streams are clearly identified and can be aggregated for reporting when required.

Step 3 – Align expenditure monitoring with enrolment developments
As enrolments evolve, MOE periodically reviews expenditure per student using internal data to understand trends in resourcing levels.

Step 4 – Use existing performance and quality assurance frameworks
Any moves to link a portion of institutional funding to performance indicators are considered within existing university performance or quality assurance frameworks.
indicators.contact: UNESCO UIS: uis.stats@unesco.org | MOE Finance Division: moe.gov.bn | JPA: jpa.gov.bn | DEPS: deps.gov.bn
indicators.kpi: Maintain rank 6/135. University Performance Scorecard published from 2026. 20% budget allocation tied to measurable KPIs.
indicators.policy_rec: Maintain this competitive advantage while growing GER. Do not let per-student expenditure fall below current levels as enrolment expands.

indicators.name: PISA Scores (Reading / Maths / Science)
indicators.definition: Composite OECD PISA score for Reading, Mathematics, and Science — assessed for 15-year-olds. Gold standard international benchmark of school system quality and student competency.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE RESPONSIBILITY. PISA is the gold-standard international school quality benchmark. Brunei ranked 43/81 participants in PISA 2022. MOE controls curriculum and teacher training.
indicators.current_status: Score: 42.06/100, Rank: 43/135. PISA 2022 results (published December 2023). Singapore ranked 2nd globally (score 91.94). PISA 2025 CONFIRMED: Brunei participating, using 'Learning in a Digital World' cognitive domain. Results December 2026.
indicators.action_plan:
PISA 2025 CONFIRMED — Digital World Domain:
• This domain benchmarks 15-year-olds' digital problem-solving against global standards — directly linked to MOE's Digital Literacy Standards
• Conduct mock PISA digital assessments in selected schools using existing LMS platform and DRMS resources
• PISA 2025 results: December 2026 — will feed GTCI 2027; monitor digital sub-score alongside core Reading/Maths/Science

Request PISA 2022 Diagnostic Report from OECD to identify Brunei-specific weaknesses by subject and item type.

Embed PISA-style open-ended questions into SPN21 assessments — requires no new test infrastructure.
indicators.contact: OECD PISA: pisa@oecd.org | SHBIE: shbie.edu.bn | MOE Assessment Division: moe.gov.bn | MOE Curriculum Dev Dept: moe.gov.bn
indicators.kpi: Rank: 43 → 35/81 PISA participants by 2028 cycle. Score: 42.06 → 50+/100. Diagnostic report: obtained within 2 weeks.
indicators.policy_rec: PISA 2025 Digital World preparation: mock assessments in all secondary schools using existing LMS infrastructure, zero additional cost.

indicators.name: University Rankings (QS / THE)
indicators.definition: Best performance of national universities in QS World University Rankings and Times Higher Education Rankings.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE/UBD/UTB RESPONSIBILITY. University rankings reflect MOE higher education investment quality. UBD QS 2026: rank 367 (up 18). UTB now in THE. Both improving.
indicators.current_status: Score: 17.53/100, Rank: 53/135. QS 2026: UBD rank 367 (up 18 from 385). THE 2026: UBD rank 351. UTB entered THE (601+). Singapore NUS: QS rank 8. Trajectory improving.
indicators.action_plan:
Brunei should improve GTCI 3.1.5 by strengthening the ranking performance of UBD, UNISSA and UTB through:
• Data assurance — ensure all publications, citations, and staff data are correctly submitted to QS/THE annual surveys
• Targeted research support — focus on Scopus-indexed journal output in high-impact fields
• Selective international recruitment — visiting professors and joint PhD programmes
• Strategic academic visibility — UBD Open Access policy and Scopus affiliation standardisation
indicators.contact: QS Rankings: data@qs.com | THE Rankings: data@timeshighereducation.com | UBD Research Office: research.office@ubd.edu.bn | UTB Research: researchoffice@utb.edu.bn
indicators.policy_rec: Establish annual ranking data audit process — QS/THE submit data requests each year; MOE must ensure universities respond fully and accurately.

groups.label: 📚 GROUP 3: LIFELONG LEARNING & EMPLOYEE DEVELOPMENT
groups.context: Lifelong learning indicators measure whether Brunei's education system extends beyond initial schooling into continuous professional development. MOE sets the culture through CPD frameworks, university extension programmes, and IBTE corporate training.

indicators.name: Business Masters Education (MBA Rankings)
indicators.primary_source: QS Global MBA and Business Masters Rankings 2025
indicators.definition: Best performance of national business schools in QS Global MBA Rankings and FT Business School Rankings. Score=0 because no Brunei MBA programme is globally ranked.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE/UBD RESPONSIBILITY. Score=0.00, Rank 64/135. No Brunei MBA programme is globally ranked. UBDSBE must begin AACSB accreditation journey immediately.
indicators.current_status: Score: 0.00/100, Rank: 64/135. No Brunei business school in QS MBA or FT rankings. UBDSBE offers MBA but lacks research output and international faculty threshold for ranking.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Clarify current status and relevance of global MBA rankings
UBD School of Business and Economics (UBDSBE) and UTB School of Business document current enrolment, graduate employment, international students, existing accreditations, and how global rankings (QS, FT) assess programmes.

Step 2 – Begin AACSB Eligibility Application (prerequisite for QS/FT ranking eligibility)
Budget: BND 150,000–200,000. Contact: accreditation@aacsb.edu

Step 3 – Leverage targeted partnerships
Negotiate joint MBA with NUS Business School or Warwick Business School — co-badging accelerates ranking timeline. Launch UBD Islamic Finance MBA — 12-month programme combining Islamic jurisprudence, fintech, and conventional finance.
indicators.contact: AACSB: accreditation@aacsb.edu | QS MBA: businessschools@qs.com | FT Rankings: ftranking@ft.com | NUS MBA: mba.admissions@nus.edu.sg
indicators.kpi: AACSB candidacy: 2028. First QS MBA ranking: 2030. Score: 0.00 → 20+/100 by 2030.
indicators.policy_rec: AACSB accreditation pathway for UBDSBE — this is the required gateway to QS MBA ranking eligibility.

indicators.name: Employee Development Practices
indicators.definition: Business executive perception of the extent to which companies invest in training and professional development of employees.
indicators.moe_link: MOE COORDINATING ROLE. MOE shapes continuous learning culture through CPD frameworks, university extension, and civil service training partnerships with CSPS.
indicators.current_status: Score: 62.24/100, Rank: 57/135. Moderate employee development perception. Singapore ranks 11th (72.64). Private sector L&D investment lags civil service.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Support inclusion of Brunei in future enterprise training surveys
MOE works with MoFE and DEPS to signal interest in Brunei's participation in future World Bank Enterprise Surveys or similar instruments that capture firm-level training practices.

Step 2 – Position MOE providers as accessible options for employer training
L3C, IBTE and university continuing-education units prepare simple overviews of courses suitable for employed adults and share them with agencies that regularly engage employers (Darussalam Enterprise, JCB, chambers of commerce).

Step 3 – Collect light-touch data on employer-linked training activity
Within their existing reporting capacities, L3C, IBTE and universities record basic information on training delivered to employed participants and share an annual summary with MOE.
indicators.contact: WEF EOS via BEDB: eos@weforum.org | CSPS via JPA: jpa.gov.bn | PEB: peb.gov.bn | UBD Continuing Ed: ubd.edu.bn | UTB Continuing Ed: utb.edu.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 62.24 → 70/100 by 2027. Rank: 57 → 40/135. CPD Framework published: 2026. Micro-credentials: 5,000/year by 2027.
indicators.policy_rec: National CPD Framework — mandatory annual training hours for civil servants (40 hrs/year) + SkillsPlus utilisation tracking dashboard.

groups.label: 🌱 GROUP 4: ACCESS TO GROWTH OPPORTUNITIES
groups.context: Growth opportunities indicators link education outputs to employment and professional advancement. MOE role: ensure smooth school-to-work transitions, reduce NEET, and grow professional networking culture through university requirements.

indicators.name: Youth NEET Rate
indicators.definition: Youth (15–24) not in education, employment or training (NEET) as % of youth population. A critical education-to-work transition indicator. Lower NEET rate = better education-employment linkage.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE ROLE. School-to-work transition is an education outcome. Youth NEET reduction requires MOE to ensure all leavers have clear pathways into education, training, or employment.
indicators.current_status: Score: 67.31/100, Rank: 66/135. Improving trend but structural gaps in school-to-work transition remain. DEPS LFS 2025 data must be submitted to ILOSTAT. Unemployment: 4.8% in 2024. MPEC restructured July 2024 → PPTM + JCB.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Clarify the NEET picture using existing surveys
MOE Planning Unit works with DEPS to understand current NEET trends using the Labour Force Survey, disaggregated by age, gender, and education level, and to ensure timely submission of youth labour market data to ILOSTAT.

Step 2 – Improve information to students on existing pathways
Using content from the Manpower Blueprint, JCB, IBTE and scholarship schemes, MOE develops a concise 'Pathways After School' guide for Year 11 and Year 13 students.

Step 3 – Strengthen career guidance within current resource envelopes
MOE reviews current counselling coverage in secondary schools and identifies clusters where NEET risk is highest, prioritising those for enhanced group-based career guidance sessions.

Step 4 – Use existing short-course platforms as 're-entry' options
IBTE and L3C identify existing short skill programmes (hospitality, ICT, basic technical trades) that can be promoted specifically to recent school leavers not in further study or stable work.
indicators.contact: ILO ILOSTAT: ilostat@ilo.org | DEPS: deps.gov.bn | JobCentre Brunei (JCB): jobcentrebrunei.gov.bn | PPTM: pmo.gov.bn | IBTE: ibte.edu.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 67.31 → 75/100 by 2027. Rank: 66 → 50/135. NEET rate: reduce by 3 percentage points by 2028.
indicators.policy_rec: 'Pathways After School' digital guide for Year 11/13 + JCB-IBTE Graduate Placement Tracking Partnership.

indicators.name: Use of Professional Networks (LinkedIn)
indicators.definition: Penetration of LinkedIn professional networking platform among the employed workforce. Reflects digital professional culture.
indicators.moe_link: MOE ROLE. University and IBTE graduation LinkedIn requirement is the single highest-impact, lowest-cost action to grow Brunei's professional network penetration score.
indicators.current_status: Score: 36.47/100, Rank: 35/135. LinkedIn penetration among workforce. Singapore ranks 4th (80.52). MOE can accelerate growth through graduation requirements.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Understand current usage patterns with existing data
MOE requests a concise LinkedIn Economic Graph country brief for Brunei (profile counts by sector and age group), coordinated with MTIC or BEDB.

Step 2 – Integrate professional networking into existing career development activities
Rather than a stand-alone graduation condition immediately, MOE encourages UBD, UTB, UNISSA, PB and IBTE to embed 'professional digital identity' into existing career support (final-year seminars, internship briefings, career fairs).

Step 3 – Gradual adoption of institutional expectations
Based on pilot feedback and employer input, each institution decides whether to adopt a light-touch expectation that students maintain a professional online profile.

Step 4 – Promote responsible and secure use of professional networks
MOE's existing digital citizenship materials are updated to include guidance on professional networking platforms.
indicators.contact: LinkedIn Economic Graph: economicdevelopment@linkedin.com | UBD Career Services: ubd.edu.bn | UTB Career Services: utb.edu.bn | IBTE: ibte.edu.bn | MTIC: mtic.gov.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 36.47 → 50/100 by 2027. Rank: 35 → 25/135. 5,000 new complete Brunei LinkedIn profiles added annually from graduation requirement.
indicators.policy_rec: Pilot 'professional online presence' as a UBD/UTB graduation expectation from 2026 intake — measurable, zero-cost, immediate GTCI impact.

groups.label: 💼 GROUP 5: WORKFORCE EDUCATION QUALITY & SKILLS MISMATCH
groups.context: Skills mismatch and workforce education quality indicators reflect the ultimate output of Brunei's education system. Rank 119/135 on finding skilled employees (5.2.1) is a national emergency — it is Brunei's single worst GTCI score and MOE's greatest challenge.

indicators.name: Workforce with Secondary Education (Employed)
indicators.definition: Share of employed persons with at least secondary education as % of total employment. Baseline measure of workforce educational attainment.
indicators.moe_link: OUTCOME INDICATOR reflecting MOE historical output. Declining trend needs urgent investigation — may reflect positive compositional shift as tertiary graduates replace secondary-only workers.
indicators.current_status: Score: 62.63/100, Rank: 28/135. Share of employed with secondary education. Declining (-10.6 pts). Singapore ranks 117th (11.65) — Brunei outperforms Singapore here significantly.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Jointly analyse recent trends with DEPS
MOE DP and DEPS Statistics Unit conduct a short joint analysis of Labour Force Survey data to understand the recent decline, disaggregated by age, gender, sector and highest education level.

Step 2 – Use findings to shape adult secondary and bridging provision
If the analysis indicates that certain groups of working-age adults are leaving school before completing secondary education, MOE reviews existing adult education and bridging programmes.

Step 3 – Establish a light periodic review routine
MOE and DEPS agree to revisit this indicator periodically (every 2–3 years) using updated LFS data.
indicators.contact: ILO ILOSTAT: ilostat@ilo.org | DEPS: deps.gov.bn | INSEAD GTCI: gtci@insead.edu
indicators.kpi: Score: 62.63 → stabilise 65+ by correcting LFS coding. Adult completers: 500/year from 2026.
indicators.policy_rec: DEPS to verify IBTE Diploma graduates are coded correctly in LFS as ISCED Level 3 completers — miscoding artificially suppresses this score.

indicators.name: Ease of Finding Skilled Employees
indicators.definition: Business executive perception of ease of finding skilled employees in the local labour market. Brunei ranks 119/135 — its single worst GTCI indicator.
indicators.moe_link: MOST CRITICAL MOE INDICATOR — Rank 119/135. Brunei near-LAST globally on finding skilled employees. MOE produces the graduates employers cannot find. This is the single most urgent education reform issue in Brunei.
indicators.current_status: Score: 42.88/100, Rank: 119/135. Employers report graduates lack practical skills, problem-solving, and industry readiness. Singapore ranks 57th (58.33). Graduate employment within 6 months: 76% (2022, up from 67% 2021).
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Strengthen the use of Industry Advisory mechanisms already in place
MOE requests all public HEIs to document their existing industry advisory committees (membership, meeting frequency, key recommendations) and to share an annual summary with MOE.

Step 2 – Embed mandatory internship components in all degree programmes
12-week minimum industry placement for all UBD and UTB undergraduate degrees — model on existing UTB engineering internship framework.

Step 3 – Launch Annual National Skills Demand Assessment aligned to Manpower Blueprint 2024
MOE and PPTM co-produce annual skills gap report; feed directly into curriculum review cycle.

Step 4 – WEF EOS Respondent Briefing
BEDB to brief Brunei WEF EOS respondents on MOE curriculum improvements, graduate employment data, and internship programmes before each annual survey round.
indicators.contact: WEF EOS via BEDB: eos@weforum.org | Darussalam Enterprise: de.gov.bn | UBD: ubd.edu.bn | UTB: utb.edu.bn | JTK: labour.gov.bn | JCB: jobcentrebrunei.gov.bn | PPTM: pmo.gov.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 42.88 → 55/100 by 2027. Rank: 119 → 90/135 by 2027; 70/135 by 2030. Graduate-employer satisfaction: 75% by 2027. THIS IS MOE'S MOST CRITICAL GTCI TARGET.
indicators.policy_rec: Statutory Industry Advisory Boards for all HEIs + Mandatory 12-week internships (all degree programmes) + Annual National Skills Demand Assessment. Three interventions, one strategic priority.

indicators.name: Relevance of Education System to Business Needs
indicators.definition: Business executive perception of whether the education system meets the needs of a competitive economy.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE RESPONSIBILITY. Education-business relevance is a curriculum governance issue. Brunei ranks 26/135 — relative strength. Must improve further to close Singapore gap (rank 3).
indicators.current_status: Score: 60.78/100, Rank: 26/135. Singapore ranks 3rd (77.26). Business executives rate Brunei's education-business relevance 26th globally. Gap to Singapore: 16.5 points.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Strengthen existing channels for structured employer feedback
MOE maps existing employer engagement mechanisms (faculty advisory panels, MISC, sector councils, internship feedback, JCB employer outreach) to understand what is already in place.

Step 2 – Strengthen the EES (Education-Employment Scorecard) Report to ensure relevancy of curriculum to workforce needs.

Step 3 – Embed career understanding in existing school curriculum and guidance
As SPN21 reviews continue, MOE Career Guidance Unit ensures that career awareness and labour-market information are progressively integrated into existing subjects and school-based career guidance.
indicators.contact: WEF EOS via BEDB: eos@weforum.org | BCC: bcc.org.bn | Darussalam Enterprise: de.gov.bn | MOE Curriculum Dev Dept: moe.gov.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 60.78 → 70/100 by 2027. Rank: 26 → 15/135. Programme Relevance Index (PRI) average: 3.5/5.0 across all programmes by 2027.
indicators.policy_rec: Annual Programme Relevance Review — all HEI programmes reviewed against Manpower Blueprint every 3 years, with employer sign-off required for continuation.

groups.label: 🔬 GROUP 6: RESEARCH, INNOVATION & KNOWLEDGE OUTPUT
groups.context: Research and knowledge output indicators measure whether Brunei's universities are generating new knowledge. With researcher density at rank 73 and R&D spending at rank 80, MOE must urgently invest in research infrastructure and reporting.

indicators.name: R&D Expenditure (% of GDP)
indicators.primary_source: UNESCO Institute for Statistics, WIPO GII, OECD MSTI
indicators.definition: Gross domestic expenditure on Research & Development as a % of GDP. Measures the intensity of investment in knowledge creation across public, higher education, and business sectors.
indicators.moe_link: CRITICAL MOE COORDINATION ROLE. R&D expenditure flows through UNESCO UIS which MOE manages. UBD and UTB are primary R&D institutions. Much R&D spending may exist but is not formally reported.
indicators.current_status: Score: 4.29/100, Rank: 80/135. R&D ~0.1% of GDP. Far below UNESCO recommended 1% and ASEAN average ~1%. Singapore: rank 19 (score 29.04). Reporting gap likely inflates the true deficiency.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Map existing R&D spending using current government systems
MOE (through the Higher Education / Research Division) works with DEPS, MoFE and relevant agencies to compile an overview of public R&D-related allocations using existing budget and programme documents (development estimates, BNRC grants, sectoral research projects), guided by Frascati Manual definitions.

Step 2 – Clarify how Brunei's R&D data are currently submitted internationally
MOE and DEPS jointly review how Brunei's R&D figures are reported to UNESCO UIS, WIPO and any other relevant international databases, and confirm which institution is the formal national focal point.

Step 3 – Improve internal classification of research-related spending
In collaboration with MoFE and key line ministries, MOE encourages clearer tagging of research-oriented items in existing budgets (CREATES competitive grants, university research centres, sectoral pilot projects).

Step 4 – Use the consolidated picture to inform policy discussions
Once an initial national R&D expenditure profile is available, MOE prepares a concise note for senior leadership summarising current levels and how Brunei compares to regional peers.
indicators.contact: UNESCO UIS: uis.stats@unesco.org | OECD MSTI: STI.stats@oecd.org | WIPO GII: gii@wipo.int | BNRC: via moe.gov.bn | DEPS: deps.gov.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 4.29 → 15/100 by 2027 via comprehensive reporting. Score: 30+ by 2030 with 0.5% GDP investment. First complete UNESCO R&D submission: June 2026.
indicators.policy_rec: National R&D Expenditure Audit covering UBD, UTB, Shell Brunei Technology Centre, and EVPMO — submit to UNESCO UIS Form S by June 2026.

indicators.name: Researcher Density (FTE per 1,000 Labour Force)
indicators.primary_source: WIPO GII 2025; UNESCO UIS; OECD MSTI Database
indicators.definition: Number of researchers in full-time equivalent (FTE) per 1,000 persons in the labour force. Brunei ranks 73rd — critically low.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE RESPONSIBILITY. Score 4.82/100, Rank 73/135 — critically low researcher density. MOE controls universities (the primary researcher employers) and UNESCO R&D Personnel data submission.
indicators.current_status: Score: 4.82/100, Rank: 73/135. Researcher FTE per 1,000 labour force ~0.2–0.3. Singapore: rank 4 (score 82.79). Gap is ~17x. Data under-reporting likely inflates the actual gap.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Clarify definitions and current reporting arrangements
MOE, working with DEPS and the designated national R&D statistics focal point, reviews UNESCO UIS guidance on R&D personnel (Form R) to agree on a common national definition of 'researcher'.

Step 2 – Collect basic researcher headcount data using existing structures
MOE coordinates with UBD, UTB and UNISSA to obtain annual counts of academic and research staff who meet the agreed researcher criteria, by broad field and employment category, using internal HR and research office records.

Step 3 – Improve the quality and consistency of submissions to UNESCO UIS
Using the compiled data, DEPS submits updated researcher figures to UNESCO UIS in the standard format, with a short technical note explaining coverage, definitions and any limitations.

Step 4 – Use evidence to inform gradual strengthening of the research workforce
Once an initial baseline is established, MOE discusses with UBD, UTB and other key institutions how their existing academic staffing and postgraduate plans contribute to building a sustainable research workforce.
indicators.contact: UNESCO UIS: uis.stats@unesco.org (Form R: R&D Personnel) | WIPO GII: gii@wipo.int | UBD Research: research.office@ubd.edu.bn | UTB Research: researchoffice@utb.edu.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 4.82 → 12/100 by 2027. Rank: 73 → 60/135. Researcher FTE: 0.2 → 0.8 per 1,000 labour force by 2030.
indicators.policy_rec: National Researcher Census — MOE to coordinate UBD, UTB, UNISSA, government research units. Submit UNESCO UIS Form R by June 2026. Data ALREADY exists in HR systems.

indicators.name: Soft Skills in Workforce
indicators.definition: Business executive perception of soft skills (communication, critical thinking, creativity, adaptability) among the workforce. Originates in school and university curriculum quality.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE ROLE. Soft skills originate in school and university curriculum. MOE controls both the SPN21 framework and university course requirements.
indicators.current_status: Score: 57.13/100, Rank: 60/135. Singapore ranks 20th (68.19). Brunei private sector consistently cites soft skills gaps — particularly communication, critical thinking, and professional behaviour.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Make soft skills more explicit in SPN21 and school assessments
As part of ongoing SPN21 reviews, MOE Curriculum Development Department works with subject panels to make expectations around communication, problem-solving, collaboration and creativity more explicit in learning outcomes.

Step 2 – Strengthen professional and soft skills in higher education
MOE discusses with UBD, UTB and UNISSA how to ensure that all programmes give students structured exposure to professional and workplace skills, either through a common module or through embedded components within existing courses.

Step 3 – Build teacher capacity for soft-skills pedagogy using existing training structures
SHBIE and MOE's in-service training units gradually integrate soft-skills pedagogy into pre-service programmes and selected continuous professional development courses.
indicators.contact: WEF EOS via BEDB: eos@weforum.org | SHBIE: shbie.edu.bn | MOE Curriculum Dev: moe.gov.bn | UBD: ubd.edu.bn | UTB: utb.edu.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 57.13 → 65/100 by 2027. Rank: 60 → 45/135. 100% of UBD/UTB programmes include Professional Skills module by 2026.
indicators.policy_rec: Mandatory Professional Skills module (communication, critical thinking, teamwork, problem-solving) in all UBD and UTB degree programmes from 2026 intake.

indicators.name: Scientific Journal Articles (Publications)
indicators.primary_source: World Bank WDI / National Science Foundation
indicators.definition: Scientific journal articles produced per million population. Measures the knowledge creation and research output capacity of the education and research system. Brunei rank 37 (+8.62 pts).
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE/UBD/UTB RESPONSIBILITY. Scientific publications are a direct university research output. Rank 37/135 (+8.62 pts improvement) — a growing strength MOE must sustain and accelerate.
indicators.current_status: Score: 32.91/100, Rank: 37/135, improving (+8.62 pts). One of Brunei's best knowledge output indicators. UBD is the primary driver. Singapore ranks 5th (81.54). Central APC Fund is the single highest-impact intervention.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Establish a shared picture of current publication output
UBD, UTB and UNISSA compile annual data on peer-reviewed publications indexed in major databases (Scopus, Web of Science), disaggregated by field and type, and share a consolidated summary with MOE.

Step 2 – Improve the accuracy of international reporting
University research offices agree on a standard institutional affiliation format (e.g. 'Universiti Brunei Darussalam (UBD), Brunei Darussalam') and communicate this clearly to staff and postgraduate researchers to reduce fragmentation of Brunei outputs in databases.

Step 3 – Consolidate and rationalise existing publication support mechanisms
Subject to available resources, MOE discusses with universities how current support schemes for conference participation, open-access fees and research assistance can be made more transparent and targeted.

Step 4 – Encourage focused collaboration to build sustainable research capacity
Universities identify a limited number of strategic research areas (biodiversity, health, renewable energy, Islamic finance, education) where international collaboration is likely to yield both capacity-building and publications.
indicators.contact: UNESCO UIS: uis.stats@unesco.org | Scopus: scopus.com/sources | UBD Research: research.office@ubd.edu.bn | UTB Research: researchoffice@utb.edu.bn
indicators.kpi: Score: 32.91 → 45/100 by 2027. Rank: 37 → 28/135. Scopus publications: 500/year by 2027 (from ~250). APC Fund: approved by Jan 2026.
indicators.policy_rec: UBD Article Processing Charge (APC) Fund — BND 500,000/year for open-access energy research publications. UBD Scopus affiliation standardisation to recover missing publications.

groups.label: 🌐 GROUP 7: INTERNATIONAL OPENNESS & SOCIAL EQUITY (Education Role)
groups.context: International student attraction, social mobility, and gender equity in education are indirect but important education outcomes. MOE plays a key enabling role through curriculum design, scholarship administration, and institutional culture.

indicators.name: International Students in Tertiary Education
indicators.primary_source: UNESCO Institute for Statistics / WIPO GII
indicators.definition: Internationally mobile students enrolled in the country's tertiary institutions as a % of total tertiary enrolment. Proxy for higher education internationalisation and global attractiveness.
indicators.moe_link: DIRECT MOE/UBD RESPONSIBILITY. International student numbers fall under MOE governance. UNESCO UIS submission and scholarship programmes are MOE-controlled.
indicators.current_status: Score: 15.76/100, Rank: 53/135. Brunei attracts few international students. Singapore data not reported for this indicator in GTCI 2025. Brunei's high per-student spending creates good infrastructure but minimal international marketing.
indicators.action_plan:
Step 1 – Establish a common definition and baseline
MOE Higher Education Division shares the working definition of 'international student' consistent with UNESCO UIS guidance (non-citizens enrolled in Brunei-based tertiary programmes, excluding short visits).

Step 2 – Ensure accurate and consistent reporting to international statistical systems
DEPS, as the national statistics authority, incorporates verified international student numbers from MOE and institutions into submissions to UNESCO UIS.

Step 3 – Align international student recruitment with existing university strategies and national priorities
Rather than setting large numerical targets, MOE encourages universities to integrate international student recruitment into their existing strategic plans, focusing on programmes where there is clear demand and capacity (Islamic finance, biodiversity, Malay language and culture, engineering, teacher education).

Step 4 – Use partnerships and regional initiatives already in place
Universities are encouraged to deepen participation in established regional and international schemes (ASEAN university networks, existing MoUs, credit transfer programmes).
indicators.contact: UNESCO UIS: uis.stats@unesco.org | Studyportals: studyportals.com | UBD International Office: ubd.edu.bn | JTK: labour.gov.bn | ASEAN University Network: aunsec.org
indicators.kpi: Score: 15.76 → 25/100 by 2028. Rank: 53 → 40/135. International students: 500 → 1,500 by 2030.
indicators.policy_rec: UBD/UTB International Student Recruitment Strategy 2026–2030 — focus on ASEAN neighbours and OIC member states, leveraging Islamic education brand.
